g.data-narration: Welcome to Wind Energy - one of the fastest-growing renewable energy sources in the world today.
Wind energy comes from moving air. Wind turbines capture the kinetic energy in wind and convert it to electricity.
g.text: Energy derived from the kinetic energy of moving air masses, converted into electrical
energy using wind turbines. Produces no harmful emissions and relies on an
inexhaustible natural resource. Accounts for ~6% of global electricity generation.

g.data-narration: Wind energy dates back thousands of years. Ancient civilizations used windmills for grinding grain. Modern wind power began in the 1970s.
g.text: • Ancient: Windmills for grinding grain, pumping water
• 1970s: Modern wind energy response to oil crises
• Today: Cornerstone of sustainable energy transition
• Major states: TN, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Rajasthan

g.data-narration: The fundamental wind power equation is P = ½ρAV³. Power is proportional to the cube of wind velocity, making speed critical.
g.text: Where: P = Power (W), ρ = Air density (1.225 kg/m³), A = Swept area (πr²), V = Wind velocity (m/s)

g.data-narration: The Betz Limit states that no wind turbine can exceed 59.3% efficiency. Modern turbines achieve 35-45% efficiency.
g.text: German physicist Albert Betz (1919) proved that wind turbines cannot capture more than 59.3% of wind's kinetic energy.

g.data-narration: Wind turbines are classified as Horizontal Axis (HAWT) or Vertical Axis (VAWT). HAWTs are most common for grid power generation.
A wind energy system has key components: rotor blades, nacelle, gearbox, generator, tower, foundation, and control systems.
g.text: Components of Wind Energy Conversion System

g.data-narration: Wind resource assessment involves measuring wind speed over 1-3 years, analyzing data with Weibull distribution, and determining power density.
Example: A 80m diameter turbine at 7 m/s wind speed. Calculate swept area, theoretical power (~1.056 MW), and actual power (~422 kW).
g.text: Given: Diameter = 80m, V = 7 m/s, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³, Cp = 0.40

g.data-narration: Example: A 2 MW turbine costing Rs. 12 crores operates at 30% capacity factor. Annual revenue is Rs. 2.1 crore with ~6.45 year payback.
g.text: Given: 2 MW turbine, Cost = Rs. 12 crore, Capacity factor = 30%, Rate = Rs. 4/kWh, O&M = 2%

g.data-narration: A 16.7% wind speed increase (6 to 7 m/s) results in 58.9% power increase! This demonstrates the critical V³ relationship.
g.text: Since P ∝ V³, increasing wind speed from 6 m/s to 7 m/s:

g.data-narration: India has major wind farms: Muppandal (1500 MW), Jaisalmer (1065 MW), Bhuj (500 MW). Government provides incentives like accelerated depreciation.
g.text: Wind Energy in India - Key Projects
Incentives: Accelerated depreciation, Tax holidays, Preferential feed-in tariffs

g.data-narration: Remember these key formulas: P = ½ρAV³ for power, Betz limit is 59.3%, and swept area is πr².
